storm.summary_layperson: No thunderstorms are threatening today — radar is completely clear, and the Sound's cooler water adds an extra buffer even on this hot, humid afternoon.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water at 74°F vs. air near 88°F creates a moderate marine layer that tends to weaken or deflect any approaching storm before it reaches the cove.
storm.detail_html:
Regional picture: KOKX radar shows no storm cells anywhere within the 50-mile ring — the afternoon is completely clear of convective activity. No severe weather warnings are in effect; the active alerts are a Heat Advisory (through Tuesday evening) and an Air Quality Alert (expiring tonight at 11 PM EDT), both unrelated to storm risk.

Instability and cap: CAPE is 1,210 J/kg — moderate, and enough to support isolated convection in theory. However, NWS OKX's afternoon Area Forecast Discussion explicitly notes a capping warm layer near 850 mb is suppressing convective initiation today, with just 15–30% PoPs. CAMs are split on whether anything fires this evening. Tonight stays dry.

Local storm modifier: The Sound's protective mechanism is active: water 73.9°F vs. inland air 88°F = 14°F differential, producing a moderate stable marine layer over the Sound. If a rogue cell did approach from the SW–NW, this layer would work against it — CAPE of 1,210 J/kg is well below the 3,000 J/kg punch-through threshold. Any cell crossing the Sound would tend to weaken or split before reaching the cove. Shear at 34.2 kt is at the upper edge of the marginal zone, but with no cells in play this is academic today.

Outlook: The bigger risk window opens Thursday–Sunday when a parade of shortwaves is expected to bring showers and thunderstorms, per the AFD. Today and tonight: clear and hot. Temperatures near 90°F with heat index around 97°F are the dominant headline.

What would change this call: A pop-up cell developing inland to the NW and approaching overland — bypassing the Sound buffer entirely — would need immediate attention. CAPE rising above 1,500 J/kg in a later model cycle would also narrow the margin.

wind.tactics_blurb: The mature WSW sea breeze is oscillating ±10° around 245° — clean pressure throughout the cove with no strong persistent bias. Start mid-line and tack on headers; the SW gradient keeps the breeze honest through 9 PM.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime: A+ (thermal with synoptic tailwind). DeltaT at 13.9°F (KHPN 87.8°F inland vs. Sound water 73.9°F at Bridgeport Harbor) is a strong thermal driver — well above the 5°F gate. The synoptic gradient is adding rather than opposing: RYC showing WSW 12.2 kt, KBDR WSW 9 kt, authoritative ensemble mean 11.4 kt — comfortably inside the A+ window (onshore gradient ≤15 kt). The A+ modifier means the breeze will hold 30–60 minutes past normal thermal collapse rather than dying abruptly at sunset.

Direction — model/obs correction applied. HRRR forecasting SW 224° but the western-LIS ensemble (RYC 247°, lis_zones.west median 241°) is already 17° more veered — the predictable afternoon maturation rightward shift of the western-LIS sea breeze. Maturation correction +15° applied; trust_obs_over_model=true. Current call: observed WSW 241°. Forecast hours: corrected WSW 239–250°. Raw HRRR SW direction is behind the actual breeze — do not use it.

Cove geography at WSW 240–250°: This is the cleanest of all racing directions for the cove — flow comes straight in from the open Sound with slight channeling along the west shore. Pressure is fairly uniform from the start line to the windward mark, with a touch more weight on the eastern side (slightly more exposed to the Sound fetch). No significant shelter from Greenwich Point or the Belle Haven bluffs at this angle.

Shift character: The SB has been building since midday and is in full mature phase by race start. A mature SB oscillates ±10–15° around the mean rather than trending persistently. Ensemble direction_agreement at 0.79 and APRS CV at 0.84 are consistent with a well-established SW/WSW regime and normal summer afternoon mesoscale noise — no boundary signature. The oscillations are tactical: play them, don't commit to a side.

Speed and collapse timing: Peak speeds 11–12 kt at 17:00–18:00 EDT, then a gradual softening. Sunset ~20:12 EDT — thermal component fades from about 19:30. HRRR shows the decline cleanly: 11.3 kt at 19:00 → 9.6 kt at 20:00 → 8.9 kt at 21:00 → est. 7 kt by 22:00. On an A+ day the SW gradient prevents the sharp cliff a pure-thermal day would show. By 9 PM it's gradient-only at ~8–9 kt, still raceable for most classes. The 22:00 estimate is from the HRRR extended outlook (lower confidence).

Fleet impact: 10–12 kt WSW is the sweet spot for one-design racing — powered up, tactical variety, no survival mode. The oscillating mature SB rewards tacking on headers. No strong persistent bias to either side; start mid-line, tack on the first header, keep pressure assessments symmetric through the beat. If racing continues past 21:00 expect a notch softer but direction holds WSW.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 met obs stale (75 min old) — sea-breeze depth estimate is approximate
KFRG anemometer offline — wind data excluded from ensemble
22:00 wind estimate from HRRR extended outlook (f7) — lower confidence than primary f01–f06 window
